summary_text:
S:
The patient reports chest pain, describing it as aching and radiating to their neck. He also reports difficulty stopping pooping and a feeling of heaviness. The patient denies shortness of breath. The patient reports elbow pain and arm pain.

O:
The patient's blood pressure was elevated. The patient's heart rate is 88 bpm and regular. The patient reports pain in their left elbow and left arm. The patient reports pain in their neck.

A:
The patient's symptoms are concerning for possible heart attack. The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes. The patient reports anxiety.

P:
An EKG will be ordered. Aspirin will be administered. Nitroglycerin will be administered. The patient will continue metformin at a dose of 500 mg. The patient will continue norvasc at a dose of 2.5 mg. The doctor ordered a blood pressure cuff for the patient.
reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Left arm pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Zachary Wood is a 45-year-old right-hand-dominant male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of left arm pain. The onset of his pain began yesterday when he woke up with pain. He denies any specific injury. The patient locates his pain from his left elbow up all the way to his neck. He describes his pain as an achiness. The patient has been taking Motrin for pain, which does not provide him with relief. He also reports intermittent chest pain that radiates up his neck. He notes that he had to stop cutting the grass the other day because of chest pain. The patient denies any shortness of breath.

For his hypertension, the patient states that his blood pressure has been around 125/72 recently. He is still taking 2.5 mg of Norvasc.

Regarding his type 2 diabetes, his last hemoglobin A1c was 7.2. He takes metformin 500 mg. The patient states that he does not check his blood glucose levels regularly.

MEDICAL HISTORY

Patient reports that he has a past medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes.

MEDICATIONS

Patient reports that he currently takes metformin 500 mg and Norvasc 2.5 mg.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Cardiovascular: Reports chest pain.
Respiratory: Denies shortness of breath.
Musculoskeletal: Reports left elbow, arm, and neck pain.

VITALS

The patient's blood pressure today is 150/90.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: Slight murmur detected, not heard before.

ASSESSMENT

Left arm pain.
Type 2 diabetes.
Hypertension.

PLAN

Regarding his left arm pain, I have explained to him that his symptoms are likely a mild heart attack. I have recommended that we obtain an EKG for further evaluation. I have also recommended that the patient take aspirin and nitroglycerin for pain. The patient will follow up with me once the EKG results are available for review and further discussion.

In regards to his diabetes, the patient will continue the 500 mg of metformin for now.

For his hypertension, the patient will continue the 2.5 mg of Norvasc.

sections.heading: S
sections.section_value: The patient reports chest pain, describing it as aching and radiating to their neck. He also reports difficulty stopping pooping and a feeling of heaviness. The patient denies shortness of breath. The patient reports elbow pain and arm pain.

sections.heading: O
sections.section_value: The patient's blood pressure was elevated. The patient's heart rate is 88 bpm and regular. The patient reports pain in their left elbow and left arm. The patient reports pain in their neck.

sections.heading: A
sections.section_value: The patient's symptoms are concerning for possible heart attack. The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes. The patient reports anxiety.

sections.heading: P
sections.section_value: An EKG will be ordered. Aspirin will be administered. Nitroglycerin will be administered. The patient will continue metformin at a dose of 500 mg. The patient will continue norvasc at a dose of 2.5 mg. The doctor ordered a blood pressure cuff for the patient.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pain in their left elbow and left arm.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a patient-reported symptom and belongs in the Subjective section; placing it in Objective could mislead a reader into thinking it was an exam finding.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient reports pain in their neck.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Neck pain here is clearly reported by the patient, not documented as a physical exam finding, so it should be in the Subjective section.

section_miscategorizations.text: The patient has a history of hypertension and diabetes.
section_miscategorizations.reason: Past medical history is typically documented in the Subjective portion; its presence in Assessment is more organizational than misleading but not ideal.
This is explicitly framed as a patient-reported symptom rather than the clinician's diagnostic impression, so it belongs in the Subjective section.
